quiz.title: Short-Run Phillips Curve
quiz.subtitle: Unit 10 Quiz 1: Inflation-Unemployment Trade-off
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: First Year - SRPC Trade-off, Shifts, Expectations, Supply Shocks

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve suggests a trade-off between:
questions.explanation: The SRPC shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment in the short run.

questions.question: Along a stable short-run Phillips curve, a decrease in unemployment is associated with:
questions.explanation: Moving down the SRPC (lower unemployment) corresponds to moving right along the AS curve, which means higher inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a movement along the short-run Phillips curve?
options: A rise in the natural rate of unemployment
questions.explanation: Changes in AD move the economy along the existing SRPC. Supply shocks or expectation changes shift the curve itself.

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve is downward sloping because:
options: Nominal wages adjust slowly to price changes
questions.explanation: Sticky nominal wages mean higher prices reduce real wages, leading firms to hire more and lower unemployment.

questions.question: If the central bank increases the money supply unexpectedly, in the short run:
questions.explanation: Unexpected expansionary policy increases AD, moving the economy up along the SRPC to lower unemployment and higher inflation.

questions.question: A reduction in aggregate demand will, according to the SRPC:
questions.explanation: Contractionary AD reduces inflation and raises unemployment — a move down along the SRPC.

questions.question: The trade-off shown by the short-run Phillips curve exists primarily because of:
questions.explanation: Sticky wages and prices allow demand changes to affect real output and unemployment in the short run.

questions.question: Which event would shift the short-run Phillips curve to the right?
options: A fall in the natural rate of unemployment
questions.explanation: Higher expected inflation shifts the SRPC right (higher inflation at any unemployment rate).

questions.question: If actual inflation is higher than expected inflation, the unemployment rate will be:
questions.explanation: When actual > expected, workers are surprised, real wages fall, firms hire more, and unemployment drops below the natural rate.

questions.question: In the short-run Phillips curve model, expected inflation is treated as:
options: A determinant of the curve's position
questions.explanation: Expected inflation determines where the SRPC is located. Higher expected inflation shifts the curve upward/rightward.

questions.question: A positive supply shock that lowers production costs will:
options: Cause a movement up the SRPC
Cause a movement down the SRPC
questions.explanation: Lower costs reduce inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC left (or downward).

questions.question: According to the short-run Phillips curve, policymakers can permanently reduce unemployment by:
options: No, not in the long run — only temporarily
Reducing the money supply growth rate
questions.explanation: Any unemployment below the natural rate requires ever-rising inflation if attempted permanently. The trade-off is short-run only.

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve implies that expansionary policy leads to:
questions.explanation: Expansionary policy can push unemployment below the natural rate temporarily, but not forever without accelerating inflation.

questions.question: If the public forms adaptive expectations, an unexpected increase in aggregate demand will initially:
questions.explanation: With adaptive expectations, people base forecasts on the past. An unexpected AD increase surprises them, lowering unemployment while raising inflation.

questions.question: The slope of the short-run Phillips curve becomes steeper if:
questions.explanation: More wage flexibility means prices affect real wages less, so unemployment changes little for a given inflation change — a steeper SRPC.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a reason the SRPC slopes downward?
questions.explanation: Perfectly flexible forward-looking wages would eliminate the trade-off entirely, so this does not explain a downward-sloping SRPC.

questions.question: A rise in the natural rate of unemployment will:
options: Shift the long-run Phillips curve right but not affect SRPC position
Shift both the LRPC and SRPC right
questions.explanation: A higher natural rate shifts the LRPC right. The SRPC also shifts right because the vertical LRPC anchors the SRPC at a higher unemployment rate.

questions.question: In the short run, if actual inflation is 5% and expected inflation was 2%, unemployment is likely:
questions.explanation: Actual > expected surprises workers, lowering real wages and reducing unemployment below the natural rate in the short run.

questions.question: The short-run Phillips curve is most useful for analyzing:
questions.explanation: The SRPC models short-run choices between inflation and unemployment, especially for stabilization policy analysis.

questions.question: A sudden rise in inflationary expectations without any change in actual current aggregate demand will:
options: Move the economy up along the SRPC
Move the economy down along the SRPC
questions.explanation: Higher expected inflation shifts the entire SRPC right (or upward). Actual inflation need not change immediately unless AD responds.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You clearly understand the SRPC trade-off, why it slopes downward, and what causes shifts vs. movements.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review the difference between movements (AD changes) and shifts (expectations or supply shocks).
scoreMessages.50: Solid effort. Focus on why sticky wages matter and how expected inflation affects the curve's position.
scoreMessages.0: Keep studying. Remember: SRPC = short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment, driven by sticky nominal wages.
